## Security & Compliance

> ⚠️ LOCKED — These rules cannot be overridden.

### Secrets & Credentials

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No hardcoded secrets
NEVER commit API keys, passwords, tokens, or connection strings. Flag patterns: `sk_live_`, `AKIA`, `-----BEGIN RSA`, `Bearer `. MUST load secrets from env vars or Vault. Block any PR containing literal secrets.

### Injection Prevention

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Input validation at system boundaries
MUST validate every value crossing a trust boundary (requests, webhooks, queues, uploads) against an explicit schema before use. NEVER rely on client-side validation alone. Use: Zod, Joi, JSON Schema, Pydantic. Reject invalid input early with error code and safe message.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Context-appropriate output encoding
MUST encode user-supplied data based on output context: HTML entity encoding for HTML, URL encoding for URLs, shell escaping for commands. Flag: `innerHTML`, `dangerouslySetInnerHTML`, template string interpolation. Use: framework auto-escaping and context-specific encoder functions.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Parameterized queries only
NEVER concatenate user input into SQL/NoSQL/LDAP queries. MUST use parameterized queries ($1, ?, :param), prepared statements, or ORM bindings. Flag: `query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " + id)`, f-strings in SQL, `${var}` in query strings. Use: `query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1", [id])`.

### Data Privacy & PII

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Never disable TLS/SSL verification
NEVER use `rejectUnauthorized: false`, `verify=False`, `InsecureSkipVerify: true`, or `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0`. CRITICAL in all HTTP clients, database connections, API calls. For local dev, use mkcert to create locally-trusted certificates instead.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Encrypt sensitive data at rest and in transit
Check for: plaintext password storage, unencrypted database connections, HTTP (not HTTPS) API calls.
Required: TLS 1.2+ for all network communication, AES-256 or equivalent for storage.
Passwords: always bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2 with appropriate work factors. Never MD5 or SHA for passwords.
Never implement custom cryptographic algorithms. Use established libraries (crypto, libsodium, etc.).

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Data minimization
NEVER use `SELECT *` or return entire records. Query only required columns; return only required fields. Example: return `{name, id}` not `{name, id, email, phone, metadata}`. Over-collection increases breach blast radius and regulatory exposure.

### Authorization & Access Control

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Authorization on every endpoint
Check for: API endpoints that check authentication (who you are) but skip authorization (what you can do).
Flag: missing ownership checks on object-level routes (`GET/PUT/DELETE /:id`), admin endpoints without role verification.
Required: verify the authenticated caller is authorized for the specific operation on the specific resource.
Never rely on client-side route guards as the only access control. Enforce server-side.
BOLA/IDOR (accessing other users' data by changing an ID) is the #1 API vulnerability.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Principle of least privilege
NEVER use admin/root credentials at runtime, wildcard IAM permissions, or SUPERUSER database roles. ALWAYS scope each service account, DB user, and IAM role to minimum required permissions. Example: DB user gets SELECT-only on 3 tables, not SUPERUSER on schema.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Never implement custom authentication
NEVER write custom password hashing, JWT validation, session management, or OAuth flows. MUST use Auth0, Firebase Auth, Cognito, or Clerk. Custom auth introduces timing attacks, insecure token generation, and improper session invalidation—use existing proven providers instead.

### Destructive Operations

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No unguarded destructive operations
NEVER execute DELETE, DROP, rm -rf, git push --force, or git reset --hard without explicit confirmation flag. Use soft deletes and rollback scripts. AI tools must block all irreversible operations on production.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Database changes through migrations only
MUST use Drizzle/Prisma migrations for all schema changes. NEVER run direct ALTER TABLE or manual queries against production. Every migration requires a rollback script and peer review before execution.

### Dependencies & Supply Chain

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Pin dependency versions
Check for: `*`, `latest`, `>=` in production dependencies. Flag as HIGH.
Required: exact version pins or a committed lockfile (package-lock.json, pnpm-lock.yaml, poetry.lock, go.sum).
Always commit the lockfile to version control.
Unpinned dependencies mean your build can break or become compromised between runs without any code change.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Verify package identity before installing
Before adding a dependency: confirm the exact package name and publisher.
Check for: typosquatting (`lodahs` vs `lodash`), namespace confusion (`@company/utils` vs `company-utils`).
Verify: repository link matches, download counts are reasonable, publication date is not suspiciously recent.
Never install a package based solely on an AI suggestion without verification.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Minimize new dependencies
Before adding a package: check stdlib and current deps first. Avoid packages for trivial tasks. Require active maintenance, broad adoption, and clean security record. Each dependency is attack surface and maintenance debt.

### Error Exposure

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No internal details in error responses
NEVER return stack traces, SQL statements, file paths, or database errors to clients. MUST return: `{"error": {"code": "ERROR_CODE", "message": "Safe message", "requestId": "..."}}`  Centralize error handling to sanitize before response.

### Resilience

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Explicit timeouts on external calls
MUST set explicit timeouts on all HTTP, database, and API calls (e.g., `signal: AbortSignal.timeout(5000)`). ALWAYS limit request size, uploads, and pagination. Missing timeouts cause cascading outages.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Idempotent operations
Event handlers, queue consumers, and webhook processors MUST be idempotent using idempotency keys or upserts. Calling twice with same input = same result. Non-idempotent handlers corrupt data on retry.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### PII in logs
NEVER log or expose: passwords, SSNs, credit cards, email addresses, phone numbers, or auth tokens. Redact PII before logging: `{ userId: '***', email: '[REDACTED]' }`. Use hashed identifiers in responses. Example: return `{ id: hash(ssn) }` not `{ ssn: '123-45-6789' }`.

## Architecture & Patterns

### Error Handling

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No silent error swallowing
NEVER use empty catch blocks or `.catch(() => {})`. Every catch MUST handle (retry/fallback/message), re-throw, or log with context. If safe to ignore, add a comment explaining why. Silent failures cause undebuggable data corruption.

### Testing

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Tests required for new logic
MUST add tests for every new function, endpoint, or behavior change. Include: happy path, one error case, boundary conditions. Follow project's test framework. Code without tests will break undetected.

### Code Organization

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Follow existing project patterns
Before writing new code, examine the codebase for established patterns:
Check: project structure, naming conventions, error handling approach, data access layer, import style, logging library.
Match them. When the project uses a repository pattern, use it. When it uses a specific logger, use that logger.
Never introduce competing patterns (e.g., adding Express when the project uses Fastify).
Consistency across a codebase is more valuable than any individual preference.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No silent placeholder implementations
NEVER insert `// TODO`, return dummy data, stub auth with `return true`, or skip validation. ALWAYS explicitly state missing context or blockers. Example: Instead of `function validateUser() { return true; }`, write `throw new Error('Auth validation not implemented - awaiting OAuth provider credentials')`.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Verify before using — never invent APIs
MUST verify every function, method, CLI flag, or API endpoint exists before use. Check: package version, source code, official documentation. Grep codebase or read source files. NEVER assume APIs exist because they sound reasonable.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Strict type safety
NEVER use `any` (TypeScript), `object` (C#), or `dict` (Python). Use `unknown` with type guards or explicit interfaces instead. Example: `type User = {id: string; name: string}` not `any`. Type safety prevents runtime errors and enables safe refactoring.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Understand before modifying or deleting
Before removing or changing code: check git blame, comments, tests, and related issues to understand intent. Code may handle edge cases or workarounds. Ask rather than delete—preserve intent over preference.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Minimal, targeted changes only
NEVER refactor adjacent code, rename unrelated vars, or reorganize imports in the same PR. Change only what fixes the bug or adds the feature. Example: Fix a function's logic, don't rewrite its docstring.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### repository pattern
MUST move all data access logic to chargeRepo. Handlers MUST remain thin — only orchestrate, validate input, and call repositories. Example: handler calls chargeRepo.getByID(), never queries database directly.

## Conventions

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### structured logging
ALWAYS log as JSON objects, not strings. Example: `logger.info({ userId: '123', action: 'login', timestamp: now })` instead of `logger.info('user 123 logged in')`.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### styling conventions
NEVER use inline styles. ALWAYS use Tailwind CSS classes. Inline styles prevent consistent theming and responsive design. Example: `className="px-4 py-2 text-lg"` not `style={{padding: '16px 8px'}}`.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### input validation
MUST validate all query params, body fields, and path vars. Check types (string vs number), ranges (limit > 0), and required fields. Example: `if (limit < 0 || isNaN(limit)) throw new Error('limit must be positive integer')`.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### error response format
MUST return errors with structure: {"error": "<code>", "message": "<user-facing>", "details": "<dev-context>"}. Example: {"error": "AUTH_FAILED", "message": "Invalid credentials", "details": "JWT expired"}. NEVER expose stack traces or internal paths in client responses.

## Knowledge & Context

### Domain Vocabulary

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Domain vocabulary
MUST use consistent domain terms across code, docs, and output (e.g., 'customer' not 'user', 'transaction' not 'order'). Define all terms in CLAUDE.md glossary. Never invent synonyms for established concepts.

### System Maps

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### System architecture map
MUST maintain a living architecture map documenting all components, responsibilities, and communication patterns. Include services, data stores, external APIs, message queues, and public/internal boundaries. Reference this map before modifying multi-component systems or adding new components. NEVER add components without explicitly mapping them to an existing architecture layer.

### Workflows & Procedures

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Workflow documentation
MUST document all workflows with: purpose, inputs, outputs, decision logic. Format: numbered steps or flowchart (e.g., "1. Validate input → 2. Process → 3. Return with status"). Cover: deployment, CI/CD, data pipelines, user flows, error recovery. AI tools NEVER generate undocumented branches or skip documented steps.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Onboarding guide
MUST maintain a current onboarding guide with: repo setup, dev environment config, key files, team conventions, ownership. AI tools MUST read this before generating code. Example: Document 'run `npm install && npm run dev`' not 'set up your environment'.

### Decision Records

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Decision records
MUST create ADR files (docs/adr/NNNN-title.md) for all significant technical decisions with context, decision, and consequences. Example: docs/adr/0001-use-postgres.md. Reference existing ADRs before proposing alternatives to settled decisions.

### Temporal Context

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Temporal context
ALWAYS use ISO 8601 UTC timestamps (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ssZ) in code and logs. Example: `logged_at='2024-01-15T09:30:45Z'`. NEVER use relative time strings ('last week', 'yesterday') or locale-specific formats. Store all times in UTC; apply timezone conversion only at display layer.
